key_takeaway: Reported penicillin allergy is often overreported; cross-reactivity with third-generation cephalosporins is low, but reaction history and IgE/severe reaction status matter.

applies_to: All drug allergies; NSAIDs; beta-lactam referral/documentation
key_takeaway: Drug allergy should be documented with drug name, reaction signs/symptoms/severity, date, and drugs/classes to avoid; suspected non-selective NSAID allergy generally means avoiding non-selective NSAIDs.
No clinically significant immune-mediated cross-reactivity between sulfonamide antibiotics and non-antibiotic sulfonamides is supported; avoid broad false sulfa allergy alerts.

applies_to: Amoxicillin, penicillin allergy, beta-lactam serious hypersensitivity
key_takeaway: Amoxicillin labeling contraindicates use after serious hypersensitivity to amoxicillin or other beta-lactam antibacterial drugs and warns about anaphylaxis on penicillin therapy.
Use as official label evidence for drug-specific allergy contraindications and warnings when OpenFDA result needs confirmation.

applies_to: Drug identity, RxCUI, class membership support
key_takeaway: Use for identifiers and class membership only after validation; do not blindly convert noisy EPC extraction into clinical allergy rules.
ACE-inhibitor angioedema is a clinically important adverse immune/bradykinin-mediated reaction that should trigger same-class review.
Heparin labels include hypersensitivity and HIT-related contraindication/warning concepts; keep heparin-specific allergy group distinct.
